David, realizing he had sinned, poured out his heart to God in prayer for mercy and grace. Where should backsliding children return, but to the Lord their God, who alone can heal them? He recorded, by Divine guidance, an account of how his heart felt toward God. Those who truly repent of their sins will not be ashamed to acknowledge their repentance. Also, he instructs others about what to do and what to say. David had not only done much, but suffered much in the cause of God; yet he turned to God's infinite mercy and depended on that alone for pardon and peace. He asked for forgiveness of sin. The blood of Christ, applied to the conscience, blots out transgression and reconciles us both to God and to ourselves. The believer desires all debts of sin to be erased and every stain removed; he wants to be completely cleansed from all his sins, but the hypocrite always holds back something secret and would have some favorite desire spared. David had such a deep sense of his sin that he constantly thought about it with sorrow and shame. His sin was against God, whose truth we deny through willful sin; we deceive him. And those who are truly penitent will always trace the roots of their sins to the source of original depravity. He confesses his original corruption. This is the foolishness that is bound in the heart of a child, that tendency toward evil and that resistance to good, which burdens those who have been made new and ruins those who have not. He was encouraged in his repentance to hope that God would graciously accept him. You desire truth in the inner person; this is what God looks for in a returning sinner. Where there is truth, God will give wisdom. Those who sincerely try to do their duty will be taught how to do it; but they will expect good only from Divine grace overcoming their corrupt nature.

Purge me with hyssop, with the blood of Christ applied to my soul by a lively faith, just as the water of purification was sprinkled with a bunch of hyssop. The blood of Christ is called the blood of sprinkling. If this blood of Christ, which cleanses from all sin, cleanses us from our sin, then we will truly be clean. He does not want to be comforted until he is first cleansed; if the bitter root of sorrow is removed, he can pray with faith, Let me have a well-grounded peace that you create, so that the bones broken by convictions may rejoice and be comforted. Hide your face from my sins; blot out all my iniquities from your book; erase them as a cloud is erased and scattered by the beams of the sun. And the believer desires renewal to holiness just as much as the joy of salvation. David now saw more than ever what an unclean heart he had, and sadly lamented it; but he realized that he could not change it on his own, so he asked God to create a clean heart within him. When the sinner feels this change is necessary and reads God’s promise to that effect, he begins to ask for it. He knew he had grieved the Holy Spirit through his sin and provoked him to withdraw. This is what he fears most. He prays that Divine comforts will be restored to him. How can we expect the joy of salvation if we doubt our interest in it? This made him weak; he prays, I am ready to fall, either into sin or despair, so support me with your Spirit. Your Spirit is a free Spirit, an independent agent, working freely. The more cheerful we are in our duty, the more constant we will be to it. Is this not the freedom wherewith Christ makes his people free, which is contrasted with the yoke of bondage? It is the Spirit of adoption spoken to the heart. God will deliver those to whom he is the God of salvation from guilt; for the salvation he is the God of is salvation from sin. Therefore we can plead with him: Lord, you are the God of my salvation, so deliver me from the dominion of sin. And when the lips are opened, what should they say but praises to God for his forgiving mercy?

Those who are thoroughly convinced of their misery and danger by sin would spare no cost to obtain forgiveness. But as they cannot make satisfaction for sin, God cannot take any satisfaction in them unless it expresses love and duty to him. The good work done in every true penitent is a broken spirit, a contrite heart, and sorrow for sin. It is a heart that is tender and responsive to God’s word. Oh, that there were such a heart in each of us! God graciously accepts this; it is equivalent to all burnt offerings and sacrifices. The broken heart is acceptable to God only through Jesus Christ; there is no true repentance without faith in him. People despise what is broken, but God will not. He will not overlook it or reject it, even though it does not satisfy God for the wrong done by sin. Those who have been in spiritual trouble know how to pity and pray for others suffering in a similar way. David was afraid that his sin would bring judgment upon the city and kingdom. No personal fears or troubles of conscience can make the soul that has received grace indifferent to the interests of the church of God. And may this be the continued joy of all who are redeemed, that they have redemption through the blood of Christ, the forgiveness of sins according to the riches of his grace.
